channel.title: Comments for page "Obskuracorps Memos Acquired in Operation BLACK ROPE"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Obskuracorps Memos Acquired in Operation BLACK ROPE"

item.title: Technical Team Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Revision 14 of this page introduced the following CSS change:

 #side-bar .scpnet-interwiki-wrapper { filter: opacity(0%) }

This code makes the translation module invisible, which violates the CSS Policy, under "Restrictions" » "What you can and can't change":

You may style the translation module (.scpnet-interwiki-frame) using CSS3 Filters or similar, but you can't remove it outright (even if it conflicts horribly and you already tried using filters).

The change has been reverted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, Junior Staff here.

Be reassured : this was just the result of a stupid drama and have never been applied officially. And it will never be.

Edit : And yes, Morhadow is right, there's several translations of this that have been made, it's just that you can't see it (and I think it would be cool to not hide the module, but that's just me). So, you definitely don't have to worry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In fact, there are several translations of this, but we can't know it instantly because the interwiki module1 is also hidden. The same for all the third law articles that use the Third Law CSS or derivates.

Footnotes
1. I don't know if it is compliant to the recent CSS policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This post goes against Create Commons License. It should be deleted or author's prohibition on translation should be repealed

The user who wrote this tale, 'greenwolf' declared as follows

"DO NOT TRANSLATE THIS WORK"

However, Creative commons license which SCP foundation follows (Reference: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)

"The licensor cannot revoke these freedoms as long as you follow the license terms."

As you can see, as long as you follow CCL, redistributing this tale to elsewhere could not be restricted. On CCL transforming this article's contents is allowed and translation should be possible as well. As a result, greenwolf's request on prohibition of translation could not coexist with CCL. Therefore this tale is not a 'CCL applicable work' else 'As long as CCL is completely followed this contents should be allowed to be translated to outside website(such as other language editions of SCP Foundation).

However, if this article is not CCL applicable, according to SCP Foundation's rules every contents must be CC-BY-SA 3.0 licensed, this tale should be deleted. If this article is CCL applicable, author's ban on translation is not possible with CCL, and if necessary, it must be repealed 'forcefully'

I definitely know Nazi is a very sensitive issue and very terrible thing that Hitler and his subordinate. However(although I have not browsed all language editions of SCP websites) I believe that none of them are Neo Nazis, and foreign SCP Fans have ability to translate this article properly.

If foundation's management rules that ban on translation is fair, I would like to bring this issue to Creative Commons Foundations and ask them for judgement.

item.title: Re: Author's Response
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that’s escalated fast…

I want to apologise that me comment created such a discussion.

But I think Green Wolf is the victim of an ongoing discussion, about the use of stereotypes.
And “Obskure Einheit” is momentarly one.

His article self is okay, the goi is the problem.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
-Deleted-
(Click wrong)

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
GreenWolf, if you had refrained from calling Grom a cryptofascist/Nazi and left the "Malicious Actions Undertaken by Members of the German Wiki" part out of this post, it would have been fine. However, you did neither of those things. You accuse others of slander, and then make wild unsubstantiated accusations of downvote brigading. If you feel you are being brigaded, resources like scipper exist that can look at when people voted and whether or not they are part of the German wiki.

Do not make claims that cannot be substantiated, or insinuate that other members are fascists because you don't like how they talk about Obskura. Calling other users Nazis is completely unacceptable.

Do not respond to closed staff posts

The discussion may continue in response to Grom's post, as long as the responses from here on out are civil.

item.title: Re: Author's Response
item.content:encoded:
I think I can elaborate:

+ show block
– hide block

First of all, calling a German a Nazi is the maximum insult for most. So would I be as short tempered as a few years ago I would run directly to the harassment team and not even respond, at least not in a comprehensible manner. But I see that you are angry and feel misunderstood, so I refrain to do so. You don't know me and I don't know you. Implying a political stance to someone you don't know is not the best reaction I think, and I did not mean to imply you being close to US imperialism and to be honest I don't see where you've seen that implication.

Second of all I would like to point out that to me it seems obvious that Rabe's English skills are roughly at early beginner level and he is having big trouble to express himself. Mine also are not the best, so please keep that in mind when reading our posts and, if possible, when responding. Please interpret our postings in our favor. Nobody is threatening or insulting you.

Third, I am sorry for this, this is a big misunderstanding; neither Rabe nor me or anybody else mean any harm, nor is any of us a Nazi, not even close. Rather the opposite. Should you run into a DE user who you think is actually meaning harm, please report them to us. We can either elaborate possible misunderstandings or take measures if necessary. Should we encounter Neo-Nazis on DE we would probably remove them.
And sorry for blocking your first post, we don't use to pin additional posts to the author's post on DE, so I assume Rabe didn't mean ill when he responded directly to your first post. This is not uncommon on DE. Once this is resolved I suggest that staff should delete this whole discussion to give you your first post back.

Regarding the SKP, there's no need to read machine translations, I am halfway through translating the hub to English. That block in the German wiki is a bit older, in the meantime I've read everything tagged "obskura" despite a few SCPs. And to be honest, it was not easy to bring me to read through this series of articles. Not because they where bad, on the contrary, they are good. But because the concept of the Obskurakorps. I will go into detail about that later.
I am aware that the SKP's concept is controversial, that is intended. I also expect a minor shitstorm every minute but I can deal with that. The reader shall think for themself. I don't want to tell people what to think, I just give them something to think about. I personally think that the reader is reached on a much more emotional level when they have to interpret an unvalued text. Readers have reported to feel uneasy when they read certain SKP-articles. That is the exact intended result. I think that the impact is much bigger when the readers see by themself that the Nazis where bad instead of telling them so, roughly spoken.
That block is meant to prevent anyone from writing anything connecting both yet, as both are incompatible right now. Nothing else. If you find someone harassing you or your teammates because of that block, report them to us please. If you find the block rather harsh I can change it.
I just have read these articles to get an own idea of what the Obskurakorps is, as I have only heard others experiences so far. I could have just taken that info and told everyone the OK/OC is just incompatible. I normally refuse to give feedback on EN because I perceive the environment and the way feedback is given and received here rather hostile. This incident seems to prove my perception. But instead of just declaring the OK/OC incompatible with the SKP I decided to try and do something about it, and as it is currently lacking a hub I gave my feedback on some of the articles about it. My goal is to find a way to make them compatible somehow.
To elaborate the SKP's concept further: it is documentary. Of course every author writes differently but when I write something I think about the true reasons for the person to do what they do. Of course it is illegal to obey an illegal order and in modern armies you usually can legally refuse orders that go against your morale. But when you are getting shot if you disobey or you think you have no other choice it puts everything in a different light. When I'm writing SKP content, I put characters in exactly that situations and let them do what I think fits their personality. The reader then can judge the character. If you are interested in what exactly I mean I can link you to SCP-188-DE once its translation is finished, it is depicting that concept best.

I appologize if it sounded as if I was badmouthing your characters or storytelling or so. Indeed it is hard to get all nouances. I was talking about the whole concept of the OK/OC especially in your teams series which is the biggest work about them and thus IMO can be seen as the primary context of the Obskurakorps. Throughout the whole series I am painfully reminded of hollywood- and comic Nazis, like in Hellboy for example. As said, there is neither a GOI- nor series hub (or I didn't find it), so my feedback is scattered among multiple articles unfortunately. As Nederbörd (who FYI is no DE user) already pointed out, that concept is not always well perceived here. Of course there are also Germans who like that style, no doubt. I personally find that concept dehumanizing though. It is common to depict Nazis as just evil villains in this concept. By dealing with black magic, being incredibly arrogant, threatining all and everybody etc. But that is not what makes Obskura and the SKP incompatible, that are just different concepts of depicting Nazis. The problem is the power level especially in the following articles. During the war the SKP is researching rather "worldly" anomalies, with minor success. The Obskurakorps is researching rather "unworldly" anomalies. Normaly that would be compatible to each other and would be a nice hook for tales or so. But their power level is way too high in relation to the SKP. While the SKP struggles to get results and with Hitlers unwillingness to accept the existance of anomalies, the OK/OC and the AOI together mobilize the occult energy seemingly equal to the output of a nuclear power plant without problem. There are other things about Obskura that bother me, like the involvement of the 1925 disbanded Thule Society, but this is not the place to discuss that I think.

Regarding the Obskurakorps' name, I am totaly fine with Obskuracorps as a translation if it is kept in mind that it would be written with k in German. It's of course up to you what form you would like to use, but I suggest using one writing in all articles featuring them. I have a problem with the name itself but that chan't be changed anyway so I won't go into detail here, you find more regarding that in the HSA-008 discussion if you want.

Regarding my feedback in general, I'm just telling you my thoughts, you can do with that what you want. You can take it into account or ignore it, it's your article. I am offering you and your teammates my help, you can take it or refuse it. I do not intend to make any implications or get personal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, now I see.

Well we could make an Obskurakorps thread in the forum to continue if you want.

item.title: Author's Response
item.content:encoded:
I've held off in responding to this thread, because I've found that it is rarely worthwhile to take the time to respond to cryptofascists. However, I've taken the time to write up a rebuttal to your posts — not because I believe it will change your mind, but to defend myself from the slander that has been levied at me here.

+ Malicious Actions Undertaken by Members of the German Wiki
- Close Section

Before I get into counter-arguments, however, I'd first like to document the abusive and malicious behaviors that have been directed at me by yourself and other members of the German Wiki, just to make it apparent to everyone reading why I consider your arguments to be in bad faith.

  • By responding directly to the top-level author post of this thread, you and Einer von Rabe have made it impossible for me to pin update posts to the top of this page, and have ensured that anyone reading this page will see your slanderous attacks before any other comments. Perhaps this was not your intention, but it was certainly the result.
  • I have been threatened by members of your site, being warned to "be carful how you use German History." [sic] Again, perhaps this was not the intention, but given the overall context, it is not a possibility that I have discarded.
  • You have attempted to portray me as a supporter of American imperialism and the atrocities committed by the American government, which is so far from the truth that anyone who knows me and my politics would find it laughable. To be absolutely clear, I am staunchly opposed to historical and present-day imperialist actions of the United States and its allies, and would portray their atrocities in exactly the same manner that I portray the Nazi regime here.
  • You have made attempts to incite a brigade on the German Wiki targeted against myself and my contest teammates, by recently adding the following text to the page of your own Nazi canon (which, from my own impression based on reading a machine translated version, appears to veer exceptionally close to Nazi apologism):

Im EN Wiki bildet sich gerade das „Obskurakorps”, eine Nazi GOI. Ich habe noch nicht viel darüber gelesen aber das was ich mitbekommen habe, klingt nach Holywood- und Comic-Nazis. In meinem Kopfkanon existieren die nicht, und in meiner Interpretation des SKP auch nicht. Wenn diese GOI sich anständig entwickelt, baue ich sie vielleicht als Konkurrent in den Hintergrund ein (die gehören dem Ahnenerbe e.V. an, das SKP dem SD bzw. RSHA bzw. der gleichen Stelle dem auch dar Ahnenerbe e.V. angehört). Ich bitte darum das zu berücksichtigen.

Which, translated into English by Google says:

The EN Wiki is currently forming the "Obskurakorps", a Nazi GOI. I have not read much yet, but what I've heard sounds like Holywood and cartoon Nazis. They do not exist in my head canon, nor in my interpretation of the SKP. If this GOI develops decently, perhaps I build it as a competitor in the background (belong to the Ahnenerbe e.V., the SKP the SD or RSHA or the same place that also belongs to Ahnenerbe e.V.). I ask you to consider that.

While this might be relatively tame on its own, given the larger context of this thread and recent attacks on members of the English wiki by reactionary groups, it is quite damning in my own mind — especially given your admission that you have not even read the works that you are criticizing. I am not sure what the English wiki's administrative policy is in this matter, but if I included in one of my own works a statement such as this which derides the articles of a foreign wiki, I am quite certain that it would not be well received.

With that out of the way, let me actually address the actual body of your complaints, as well as those of Einer von Rabe.

+ Counter-Arguments
- Close Section

First, as an overall statement of fact, I will note that one of my own contest teammates is, in fact, German. They offered significant input on the drafting of this tale, regarding both the usage of the German language and on overall tone and style — and they have not voiced any complaint about my portrayal of the Nazi regime. I make note of this solely to demonstrate the falsehood of assertions that I am somehow ignorant of the German perspective.

Second, you accuse me of using German words randomly, and solely as a means to evoke emotional associations with Nazism. While the use of German within this tale is done for stylistic effect, it is not random, and it is not meant to play on germanophobia. While this tale is framed as being an in-universe translation of a set of originally German documents, it was written in English for an English audience — therefore, the occasional use of German throughout the documents is done where there is no equivalent word or concept in the English language (such as with the rank of Reichsführer, a title unique to the SS), or where a literal translation would fail to convey the intended tone. For example, the use of Judenriese to refer to the Golem of Prague, instead of the literal translation of "Jew giant", is done to convey the amount of hatred and fear Obskuracorps has of the Golem — so great is their emotion that they invent a new word to use as a slur solely for the Golem, a sentiment which is lost if a literal English translation is used. Similarly, the terms Judenmagier and Kriegsmagier are respectively used to show the Obskuracorps' contempt of Kabbalists and their esteem for the Thule.

(As for the usage of Obskuracorps instead of the more correctly German spelling of Obskurakorps, that is merely a result of following the example of previous authors such as Clef, who have consistently used the term Obskuracorps. While I personally consider this a minor stylistic issue which can easily be explained away as the result of in-universe transcription errors by Anglophone organizations, if there is significant sentiment that it is a problem, I can try to get the Obskurakorps spelling standardized throughout Third Law.)

Moving on to the third point, you accuse me of portraying Nazis as mindless, faceless villains, which is a point that I find exceptionally baffling, as I took particular pains to ensure that the very human element of the Nazi's evil was readily apparent. I completely agree that it would be inappropriate to treat Nazis as inhuman monsters, and this piece was motivated at least in part as a reaction to such portrayals. The true horror of the Nazi regime lies in the very fact that they were humans, with human motives and emotions — and that despite this, they were still willing to commit (or unwilling to oppose) the industrialized genocide of millions of human beings. I have read the transcripts of the Wannsee Conference and of the post-war tribunals, and what is most striking and terrifying about them is not the lack of any genuine displays of remorse — after all, no one is a villain in their own mind — but rather the undeniable humanity of the people involved, twisted as it may be. There is no monster ever conceived by nature or human imagination that is more monstrous than a human being acting out of genuine belief in their own superiority. That is why depictions of Nazis allied with or driven by occult or inhuman forces are so problematic, and that is what I attempted to avoid here: the Obskuracorps are not allied with the occult, they are merely using the occult, just as every other organization in the Foundationverse does — in a world where magic is real and subject to scientific study, its usage will be as common and inevitable as chemistry. The Obskuracorps are ultimately still driven by the same motives as the rest of the Nazi regime, and the Rite of Solomon is merely another means through which their goals can be achieved, no different from a jet fighter or an atom bomb.

To this end, every in-universe memo writer in this tale is a distinct individual with their own motives, goals, and voices: Weiss is arrogant and self-assured, convinced of his own intelligence and superiority, and fully believing in the racial and racist ideology of the Nazi Party; Schmitz is obedient to the point of subservience, possessing and displaying little initiative of his own, but unwilling to attempt to deflect blame onto others — he practically grovels before Weiss after the Prague Incident, but still takes full responsibility for what happened, even though it could potentially earn the ire of one of the most powerful thaumaturges in the world; Boller is practically the banality of evil personified, having deluded himself into believing that he is just "following orders", to such an extent that he is able to unemotionally and offhandedly describe the atrocities of the Nazi regime as a mere sidenote in what he views as a typical military intelligence report; Wege is an archaeologist taking advantage of the opportunities presented by Obskuracorps and the war, perhaps not fully believing in the Nazi cause and rhetoric, but willing to play along to get support and funding — he is not nearly as certain as he claims about the provenance of the catacomb automaton, but acts confident anyways to curry favor with Weiss; and Himmler… obviously, he is fully committed to the Nazi cause, having helped orchestrate the Holocaust, and like his real-life counterpart, he maintains a firm belief in the occult — however, in the world of this canon, where the occult is actually real, he is cognizant enough to understand that he is out of his depth when it comes to real magic, and is more than willing to defer to Weiss' expertise, at least in private.

As you can see, the members of the Obskuracorps depicted here all have very human and personal motives, although I would not shy away from describing all of them as evil in one fashion or another. However, I understand that it is possible that these depictions — which rely in part on the connotations of some very specific word choice, and require some exegetical reading of the text — are nuances that could easily be missed by someone not as familiar with the English language as the intended audience.

Fourth, you claim I portray the Nazi regime as stupid, cartoonish buffoons. This is simply not the case, and I don't know how anyone could read this tale and think otherwise, because there is no nuance to miss here. Yes, the Obskuracorps makes mistakes and blunders, but they are all, universally, attributable to an institutional belief in the Nazi ideology, leading them to consistently overestimate their own competency and capability while underestimating the abilities of their enemies. Such a situation is readily apparent in the strategies and actions of the historical Nazi regime, where the arrogance of the Nazi leadership repeatedly led to catastrophic blunders that ultimately led to their downfall — the most obvious example of this is, of course, the ill-timed and poorly planned invasion of the Soviet Union, although the Deutsche Physik movement is the closest analogue to the actions of Obskuracorps — the Nazis were so adamant in their beliefs that Jews were inferior that they sabotaged their own nuclear research efforts by refusing to use the research and theories of Jewish scientists such as Albert Einstein. Likewise, while the Obskuracorps is attempting to use a ritual of Jewish origin, their institutional arrogance causes them — and specifically Weiss — to believe that they are smarter than Solomon (who is, after all, just an inferior Judenmagier in their view), which leads them to essentially ignore the instructions on what is an incredibly potent occult weapon. This is not because they are incompetent — Weiss, for instance, has already been established as being one of the most powerful and knowledgeable wizards in the world — but because they display a kind of Dunning–Kruger effect which is endemic to fascist regimes: an inability to accurately assess the strength and competency of enemy forces, because of an ideological certainty of their own inherent superiority.

This ideological arrogance is best seen in the Obskuracorps' attitude towards the Golem of Prague. At first they view it as just another artifact to be looted. When the Golem eliminates their recovery team and escapes Prague, this attitude shifts towards dismissive condescension, considering the Golem a dumb machine and attributing its escape to the intervention of the Templar Knights. As the war progresses, and the Golem continues to triumph against them, this attitude shifts towards fear and then to hate, as cognitive dissonance arises from the conflict between their own racist convictions, their need to acquire the final Key, and the undeniable evidence of the Golem's prowess. Eventually, the cognitive dissonance grows to a point where they simply ignore the Golem, in their arrogance believing that they can implement the Rite without the Key. These actions are all consistent with the documented tendencies and actions of fascists and racists facing similar dilemmas, and is another effort on my part to be as realistic as possible in my portrayal of the Nazi regime.

With the rebuttal out of the way, I'd like to delve into some concluding remarks about this situation.

+ Closing Thoughts
- Close Section

I have have spent most of this post attempting to respond to your arguments as if they were made in good faith, because to do otherwise would make any rebuttal impossible. However, I am quite convinced that this is not the case, and that you have been acting in bad faith from the start — by your own admission, you have not even actually read the works which you are criticizing. More than that, your near textbook use of the Nuremberg Defense — especially in relation to a tale which prominently features Heinrich Himmler, leader of the SS — makes me question your true motives here. It is incredibly common for cryptofascist and neonazi groups to accuse their opponents and critics of "reverse racism", despite the absurdity of this notion. If you have a problem with someone criticizing Nazis, maybe it's not because they're prejudiced against Germans — maybe it's because you are a Nazi.

On the off chance that you haven't been acting in bad faith, and that this is all just the result of a misunderstanding, I offer my sincere apologies for accusing you of fascism. Otherwise, I will leave you with the immortal words of the Dead Kennedies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The argument that all of them were following orders also doesn't track.

It's also not relevant from a legal standpoint, e.g."The Nuremberg Defense" and the wording of Article 82 of the UCMJ. Following an unlawful order is still criminal behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't know where you're getting "mindless villains" or "mindless monsters" in the context of this article from. While that is definitely a problem in media in general, it just isn't the case for this particular piece. The story this tells is of some highly competent scientists (as occultism in the SCP universe is just another form of science) trying to achieve a very specific goal in accordance to their ideologies. I really can't see where this fits into the notion of mindless villains, they are trying to use every means at their disposal to win the war, which in the SCP universe includes occult methods. That is something that is in no way limited to the Nazis within this universe, as the rest of this series and countless other articles show, occultism is utilized by pretty much every major organisation, political or otherwise. Consequently, the Third Reich is no exception.
The argument that all of them were following orders also doesn't track. Yes, for a lot of people that were involved with the atrocities commited during the era this is true. Nobody is pretending otherwise. But that was not the case for everyone. Plenty of people agreed very much with the Nazi ideology. Orders have to be given by someone, fear as to be spread by someone, indoctrination and spreading of hate has to be performed by people. They don't exist in a vacuum, some of the people involved were, by our standards, very much evil and saying everyone involved was just following orders in some form or other is frankly more insulting to the victims of the Nazi regime than any attempt at dehumanizing could be. And even if that wasn't the case, this article does not in any way attempt to dehumanize the Nazis. On the contrary, it makes a point of clearly laying out what their motivations for doing what they attempt to do are, instead of pretending they do it "because they're evil".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is getting off topic, but I mean the way in which the Nazi Party used systemic antisemitism (and to a lesser extent general racism) and racialist pseudoscience, combined with the 20th century machinery of state propaganda as a deliberate toolkit for aims such as eliminating their political enemies, recruiting the willing participation of the German population, and justifying and supporting their war of territorial aggression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry, I don't understand that as a whole. What weaponized racial identity politics? How can that be used as a weapon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally do not think that such crimes can be valued anyhow. Those tortured to death are tortured to death. It doesn't get any worse for them because their murderers are Nazis, Sovjets, CIA or some mafia thugs. Valueing their deaths serves only the living.

The point is, that when the enemy develops a weapon, you take note of its signs so that you can recognize and counter it before it is deployed again. While, yes, the same thing is true of chattel slavery or forced cultural assimilation, and many others, the signs of these weapons of the enemy are different, as are their counters.

The enemy invented factory genocide and weaponized racial identity politics in the 1940s and 30s respectively, and we would be foolish to not take note that these are now within the arsenal. Rwanda might have been different had the people who recognized weaponized identity at the time been willing and able to intervene.

One sees the signs of the latter all over, these days, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am indeed unfamiliar with Vietnamese and US anti-war propaganda. But I have never seen any US soldiers depicted as it is common for German soldiers. That's what I meant.

I'm looking forward to the last part then^^

I personally do not think that such crimes can be valued anyhow. Those tortured to death are tortured to death. It doesn't get any worse for them because their murderers are Nazis, Sovjets, CIA or some mafia thugs. Valueing their deaths serves only the living.

Therefore I prefer to leave the judgement completely to the reader. No slurs, no blurting out hateful phrases. I just tell the reader what happens or let the protagonist tell the story. With a neutral writing style the reader has to think, which apparently makes the experience much more intense and more intriguing as I am told, and the characters can bring in their actual emotions and thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nobody depicts US soldiers burning vietnamese children alive as mindless monsters I think.

You seem to be unfamiliar with the anti-war movement in the US, then. Also I imagine Vietnamese propaganda did so.

All they did lies in the capacity of human mind. Dehumanizing them also dehumanizes their victims. It lets them look like the victims of an inevitable evil. Like something extraordinary. Something uniquely bad. But it wasn't.

"Once upon a time there was a people who believed in Santa Claus, until they discovered Santa Claus was the gas man."

Yes, I am very sensitive to the concept of the mundanity of evil. I hope to address some of your concerns with my long delayed entry into this series. Trying to humanize Obskura's final hours while maintaining continuity and tone is one of the obstacles I have been struggling with, frankly.

Inhumane crimes of all sorts and all scales have been done before, during and after the war, by lots of countries including the US. Some are still doing horrible crimes against humanity today and are not even a bit ashamed of it.

Yes, to some extent. However, I think it is important to recognize that the kind of systematic industrialized genocide of the Final Solution as well as the way that the Nazi Party deliberately weaponized racial identity politics are the particular 20th century horrific innovations of the 3rd Reich. It doesn't deserve a pass for this just because other states have engaged in atrocities of various scales, before and since.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would you mind elaborating this very specific message?

I also agree with Nederbörd regarding the depiction of Nazis, and as Rabe said the use of German words seems rather random and could easily be seen like a style element intended to use German language as means to make it look more Nazi-like which is a use of our language most of us would find very, very disrespectful so I just assume that was not your intention.

As the Obskurakorps (Korps is written with k in German) is meant to be part of the SS, I suggest using SS ranks, e.g. "SS-Untersturmführer" instead of "Lieutenant" or the respective rank instead of "commander".

To further elaborate the problem Rabe, I and lots of DE users have with this kind of depiction of Nazis is, that it not only is very historically inaccurate, it is depicting them as mindless villains. As monsters. But they where not. None of them intended to do evil. Instead they did what they thought was necessary. Nobody depicts US soldiers burning vietnamese children alive as mindless monsters I think. Or the british pilots bombing German cities to kill civilians.
They where humans, nothing more. They where following orders, given by people who where also following orders. Some because they stopped thinking, some because they thought it was necessary, some because they where afraid, some because they where indoctrinated by hate. All they did lies in the capacity of human mind. Dehumanizing them also dehumanizes their victims. It lets them look like the victims of an inevitable evil. Like something extraordinary. Something uniquely bad. But it wasn't. Inhumane crimes of all sorts and all scales have been done before, during and after the war, by lots of countries including the US. Some are still doing horrible crimes against humanity today and are not even a bit ashamed of it.
This picture of Nazis that is common in US media is basically WW2 propaganda. It was meant to reach the people on an emotional level, without having to explain much and thereby entertaining the people to keep their attention and to appeal to their patriotism. Though I understand that is what works to depict Nazis in the US.
I guess our attempt would be highly controversial in the US. We'll see when more of our SKP-articles are available in the INT. I would favor if we could find ways to make articles compatible to both ways to depict Nazis in the future and I'm open for discussions thereabout.

item.title: Re: Nazi Wizards Fuck Off
item.content:encoded: Just noting that I've tossed on the brand new Third Law subtheme for the 7th Occult War. Colors are nice.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
Because of the competition?
That's okay, I was just trying to help.
Whether my criticism is fruitful is not so important for me.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded: It just isn't possible to make major stylistic changes now, even if the author wanted to.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
You say it.^^
I just want to help, that you're not putting your foot in your mouth. (Is this saying correct? I mean, „doing something and accidentally offend someone“)

And to make clear:
Yes, I’m just read this article, and, Yes, I’m not a fan of Obscuracorps, BUT instate to say: “yU artiCl is Schit! sHamEa offer you!”, I thought: “How could I help?”
As I indicated, it only my view, not the of 32 reader.

Edit: just because one person is says, he finds you article the best, doesn’t mean it’s a bad article. I have only one SCP official, so I’m new.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get what you're going for with this article and the method through which you portray them, but not only do I find Obskura to be a drag to read about, but this article going on for as long as it did killed my interest completely when I realized half-way through that the tone would be similar throughout. Stories being told through letters isn't typically a form of storytelling I'm into unless the portrayal feels fresh enough, and this didn't feel fresh to me.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
I think that's just Rabe grappling with the English language: it isn't his native tongue.

Though I believe he echoes a general sentiment throughout the German branch, where, from what I've understood, there's a great distaste for how Nazis are depicted in American media, particularly comic books and Hollywood, of which Hellboy is seen as a prime example. And since Obskura material often displays similarities to these, they're rather disliked over in DE.

Nazis and the Nazi period is generally a very touchy subject in Germany, as is expected to be handled with care. Not only are Nazis not to be glorified, but they are neither to be underestimated and represented as stupid or inept, as that would only obfuscate the frightening effectiveness and competence which allowed such an utterly dystopian system to come into being and persist. Moreover, the association of Nazis with magical occultism is generally seen as offensive among German as far as I know, as it serves to mystify them, on the one hand, and to trivialize the root of their horrendous atrocities to collusion with demonic or occult (and stereotypical) evil rather than attributing it to a very real and insidious, well-structured and thought-out ideology.

The writers of the SKP and 4R GoIs have been cautious in their representation of them so as to not obfuscate the very real nature and background of their evil, so to speak. I believe that that's what Rabe was alluding to.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
But as you said: "This isn't the German wiki", so be carful how you use German History.

I'm not sure what you're implying with this, but I'm not particularly happy with how this is phrased.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded: That's just not possible.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
All right, I'm just trying to help.
I linked the German GoI, because I think you can use the writing style, but I don't want to force you. (As I read from your last sentence.)
But as you said: "This isn't the German wiki", so be carful how you use German History.
Whether you want to use my "criticism" or not is actually the same to me, as long as you are satisfied with your work. ^^

Edit: I let this topping, as for me its not so importuned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best parts of the early Hellboy stories was their emphasis on slaying occult Nazis. This is a grand spiritual successor along the same veins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some fantastic world building here, you could really feel the arrogant stupidity of obskura and the rising hope that opposed them in the form of the GOC. And I could read about a Templar-Kabbalah alliance kicking Nazi ass all day. Hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gormogons are now canonically elite occult demolitions experts. It's canon now. 100%.

item.title: Re: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded: Obskuracorps has long been a thing on the English wiki, starting with references to them in the GOC canon. In fact, many of the major events of the 7th Occult War were already codified by the GOC canon, even if only in very vague terms. The goal of this series was to build upon those foundations and actually show the 7th Occult War in detail for the first time. Sorry if you think the German wiki has a better Nazi GOI, but this isn't the German wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, "Gormogon" is one heck of an evil sounding name to give your group, even though what little about them exists suggests they were charitable in nature. "Antimason" also sounds really cool, too.

item.title: Personal Thought about Obscura…
item.content:encoded:
To be honest:

I think Obscuracorps (Obskure Leiche?) isn't a good Nazi GoI.
Look at the German Branch: SKP (Canon, but it exist in the SCPvers too) and 4 Reich (Not Translated, so with Google) are Nazi GoI, and you get shiver how "different" they think.
You could put every other "Hollywood Villain" in place of your "German Nazi" Characters, and it would work.

But you try it, so nice.

P.S. It looks random where you use German…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a masterful article. You got the haughty, holier-than-thou tone of the Nazis perfectly. The bit about how crude and barbarous the killing of the team that initially makes contact with the golem was so ironic/hypocritical and I love it. +1 and fuck Nazis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Great worldbuilding. Really don't have a whole lot more to say than that, except that

This is indicative of an expansive and cohesive coalition within the global occult community

is a freakin' sweet line.

EDIT: I also love this.

It is a clumsy, crude weapon created by vermin to strike against the strong, but it is brutally effective in this role.

"I-It's not like I'm impressed by your magical constructs dirty juden! B-Baka!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, darn. :B I was hoping it was dudes who, like, specialized in destroying buildings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually a really obscure throwaway historical reference to a real-life secret society, believe it or not. Their wikipedia entry is here, but the TL;DR is that the Gormogons were a short-lived secret society opposed to the Freemasons, founded by a guy who got expelled from the Masons for being too radical and/or too Catholic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic writing. I love the cold open onto the keys, and the whole "we need this occult stuff, but obviously it's all inferior" hubris was well rendered. :) Can't wait to see where this goes!

antimasons of the Gormogons

Oh yeah, and this. This is a bunch of amazing gobbledygook and I want to know more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, its up.

I liked this as a draft and I like it here. The documents are immersive along the same line as Preliminary Report of J. C. Randall Upon His Party's Efforts to Catalogue the Multiverse and is a good set up for what I'm sure will be a neat set of articles.

Can't wait to see this backfire on the Nazi's ala the ending of "Raiders of the Lost Ark"

Fuck those guys up.

item.title: Technical Captain Post - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
This is not the first time I've seen you be bullheaded regarding tags.

I'm officially asking you not to police other people's tags, and to in general cool it with regards to tags.

The tag system is around to help us find stuff, not to brow-beat people with.

This is not a disciplinary action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it's not an attempt to create a format for the Obskuracorps? It's a bunch of their internal memos captured by the Foundation and shoved into a single file for convenience. A GOI format that relies on all of its documents being captured by an enemy organization isn't a very versatile format, imo. Not everything written from the perspective of a GOI needs to be a GOI format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this tagged Tale? This seems like a clear GoI format to me for the Obskuracorps. (Which does currently have a tag, which I added.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been hyped for this Doomsday since NineVolt asked me to crit some of their stuff. This is a really fucking good intro to what I know is going to be even better

+1 with all the fixin's

item.title: Nazi Wizards Fuck Off
item.content:encoded:
Fuck Nazis.

I really wish this didn't need to be said, but nothing in this tale should be considered a reflection of my political views, nor should it be considered an endorsement of Nazi ideology. I have attempted to portray the evil, racism, and fanaticism of the Nazi regime with as much accuracy as is possible for a story about Nazi wizards plotting to assemble a doomsday weapon. I feel that to do otherwise would be to diminish the true horrors of the Nazi regime and a disservice to its victims.

Now, with that out of the way, some crit credits and some explanations. Thanks to my Doomcon teammates, NineVolt, ChaoSera, and sirpudding, for assistance in drafting and editing this piece. Thanks also to TyGently, Taffeta, and Conwell for reviewing and critiquing the draft.

This is my team's first entry in the Doomsday Contest, and is the start of an arc that involves Nazi wizards, ancient rituals, secret wars, a Golem that punches Nazis to death, and cursed swords. Stay tuned for the remaining installments, which will be posted later today and tomorrow.

The Obskuracorps is the occult arm of the SS Ahnenerbe. The Thule Society are an order of German oculists allied with the Obskuracorps in the Seventh Occult War. Both have been mentioned in passing in other works that reference the Seventh Occult War, but I believe that this is the first time they've been written about in detail. If you couldn't tell, they're bad people who want to do some really bad things.

The Allied Occult Initiative is the predecessor to the GOC, formed at the start of the Seventh Occult War to combat the Obskuracorps. They have also been mentioned in the past, but like the Obskuracorps, there are very few works that have delved into their history. They're a pretty eclectic bunch, but they're the good guys by default because they're fighting Nazis. The Foundation is not a member of the AOI, but does join the Seventh Occult War on their side following the D-Day landings.

SCP-4241 is a placeholder number until our team's skip is posted. It'll be changed to point to the skip once it's posted.

Edit: Holy shit, just noticed that I managed to typo the title of the page. Whoops.

IMPORTANT NOTE FOR POTENTIAL TRANSLATORS

DO NOT TRANSLATE THIS WORK

Given the political nature of this work, the ease with which it can be misinterpreted when taken out of context, and the reliance on specific and subtle nuances of the English language to convey a very specific message, I would respectfully ask that this piece not be translated into a different language. As is evident in the discussion below, it is all too easy for the intended nuance to be lost when traversing a language barrier.
